meta.title: aix — an AI-native execution format
meta.description: A backend an AI writes in a fraction of the tokens, that a machine can prove correct, running with zero human in the loop. The AI emits a tiny verifiable spec; a fixed runtime interprets it.

hero.lead: A backend an AI can write in a fraction of the tokens of a Next.js/React stack, that a machine — not a human — can prove correct, and that runs with zero human in the loop.
hero.sub: The AI never writes if / for / functions. It writes a short declarative spec. A fixed, trusted runtime already knows how to run it — so what we verify isn't unbounded code, it's a tiny closed spec. That's why the verifier can be 100% deterministic.

pipeline.title: How it works
pipeline.specNote: the AI writes a tiny spec — not code
pipeline.runtimeNote: the runtime interprets the spec — no generated code exists
pipeline.rejected: rejected → structured error → the AI fixes itself

blog.title: A whole blog backend
blog.lead: This is the entire "source code". Three lines become auth-gated routes, owner auto-assignment, list:mine scoping, length validation, and field-level update locking.
blog.caption: examples/blog.aix

perf.title: Performance — measured, not claimed
perf.subtitle: The same CRUD backend (auth, ownership scoping, validation, field-locked updates), written two ways: idiomatic Next.js (route handlers + zod + prisma) vs an .aix spec. Reproduce it: node bench/run.mjs.
perf.tokensTitle: Tokens to define the whole backend
perf.lowerBetter: all 3 scenarios combined · lower is better
honesty: est. tokens = chars ÷ 4 — the common rule of thumb, not an exact tokenizer count. The raw character and line counts are exact, so the ratio holds under any tokenizer; the gap is an order of magnitude regardless.
The Next.js code is real and kept tight — no padding to inflate the gap.
Not measured yet: first-try pass rate and retry / human-intervention count. Those need a live LLM run — no fake numbers.
Storage is in-memory; this is a v0 proof-of-concept, not production.

verifier.title: The verifier catches bugs a human would otherwise debug
verifier.lead: examples/broken.aix has three bugs. check finds every one, with exact locations — the loop an AI closes by itself, with zero human in the loop.
verifier.caption: examples/broken.aix

usage.title: Usage
usage.step1: Machine-verify a spec — deterministic, no human needed
usage.step2: Run it as a live API on :8787
usage.liveTitle: Then hit the live API — the runtime enforces auth, ownership, and validation
usage.curl4: editing a field the spec didn't expose → locked

docs.title: aix spec
docs.subtitle: aix is a backend an AI writes and a machine verifies. A human can read it, but the primary user is the AI. Declare entities (data shape) and routes (exposed actions); a fixed runtime interprets them as a live CRUD API. No code is generated.
docs.structureBody: One declaration per line; # starts a comment. There are exactly two kinds of declaration:
docs.entityBody: Fields are comma-separated. Each field follows a fixed order so a machine can parse it 100% deterministically:

parts.:ref: for a ref type, the entity it points to

docs.routesBody: The entity must already be defined with E. Available actions:
docs.runtimeBody: The runtime interprets a verified spec like this:
runtimeRules: Auth — with auth, a request without an x-user-id header gets 401.
Owner auto-inject — on create, the owner ref field is filled from the logged-in user (it need not be in the body).
Owner resolution — the *-marked ref, else the single ref; multiple refs → AMBIGUOUS_OWNER at verify time.
list:mine — returns only rows whose owner equals the current user.
Validation — create/update enforce required, type, and max (REQUIRED / BAD_TYPE / TOO_LONG / TOO_BIG).
Field lock — changing a field not in update:[...] returns 403 FIELD_LOCKED.
Defaults — apply the = default when absent; now becomes the current ISO timestamp.
docs.errorsBody: aix check judges a spec 100% by machine. If it returns no errors, the runtime is guaranteed to run it. If it fails, it says exactly where and why — structured errors an AI reads and fixes itself.

errors.DUP_FIELD: duplicate field name in one entity
errors.BAD_REF: ref:X but X is not a defined entity
errors.BAD_DEFAULT: default value doesn't match the field type (=now only on ts)
errors.BAD_MAX: <= used on a type other than str/int
errors.MULTI_OWNER: more than one * owner in an entity
errors.NO_ENTITY: route points at an undefined entity
errors.BAD_UPDATE: update:[X] where X is not a real field
errors.NO_OWNER: list:mine but no ref field exists
errors.AMBIGUOUS_OWNER: list:mine with multiple refs and no * marker
errors.MINE_NO_AUTH: list:mine without auth — there is no "me"

footer.tagline: An AI-native execution format. The AI emits a tiny verifiable spec; a fixed runtime interprets it. No generated code, no human in the loop.
